title: Geodesics for The Weil-Petersson Metric
speaker: Tudor Ratiu
affiliation: EPFL (Ecole Polytechnique Federale de Lausanne), Switzerland

proof_stages.label: Euler-Poincare reduction and reconstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with Poincare's reduction theorem: invariant Lagrangians on Lie groups reduce Hamilton's principle to constrained variations on the Lie algebra, yielding the Euler-Poincare equations and a reconstruction procedure for the original geodesic problem.

proof_stages.label: Classical Banach model of universal Teichmuller space
proof_stages.summary: After introducing the relevant function spaces, quasiconformal maps, Beltrami equations, Bers embedding, quasi-symmetric circle homeomorphisms, Zygmund tangent space, and Hilbert-transform complex structure, the speaker identifies the usual universal Teichmuller space as a complex Banach manifold and group-like object.

proof_stages.label: Failure of the naive Weil-Petersson metric
proof_stages.summary: The formal Weil-Petersson pairing on the Banach universal Teichmuller space is shown not to converge on the natural tangent space, paralleling the failure of the Banach model to be a topological group.

proof_stages.label: Takhtajan-Teo Hilbert re-modeling
proof_stages.summary: Takhtajan and Teo's idea is to keep the same underlying set but endow it with a Hilbert manifold structure modeled on the L2 version of the tangent space, making the Weil-Petersson metric strong, Kähler-Einstein, and compatible with a connected topological-group structure.

proof_stages.label: Critical H^{3/2} regularity and below-critical calculus
proof_stages.summary: Because the tangent space sits at the critical Sobolev index H^{3/2}, the speaker reviews sharp flow regularity, introduces H^{1/2-} and H^{3/2-} topologies, and states multiplication and composition results needed to control operations below the critical threshold.

proof_stages.label: Weil-Petersson geodesics and the valid Eulerian equation
proof_stages.summary: Using the strong Hilbert Weil-Petersson geometry and the technical low-regularity calculus, the speaker states geodesic completeness and smoothness of the geodesic spray, explains why the formal Euler-Poincare equation is ill-defined at H^{3/2}, and replaces it with a weaker Eulerian evolution that is C^1 only after losing one derivative.

proof_stages.label: Applications and remaining exponential-map issue
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion applies Weil-Petersson geodesics to shape comparison by conformal welding, notes further long-time-existence consequences, and highlights the unresolved question of proving an exponential-map/flow characterization at the critical regularity.

examples.name: KdV as a Virasoro geodesic
examples.note: The Virasoro/Bott extension with the L2 metric is used as a worked case of Euler-Poincare reduction, and its coadjoint-action computation reveals the Schwarzian and points toward the universal Teichmuller space.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the Virasoro group and Lie algebra cocycles, computes the coadjoint action/geodesic equations, and identifies the resulting equation with KdV

passes.reason: KdV is used as a standard illustrative geodesic equation for the Bott–Virasoro group, not as a failure case or classification exception.
KdV is used as a standard integrable PDE arising as the L2 Virasoro geodesic equation, not to refute a claim.
KdV is used as a standard Euler–Poincaré/geodesic example on the Bott–Virasoro group, not as a refutation or sporadic special case.
KdV is a completely integrable soliton equation with large Virasoro/Hamiltonian symmetry, used here as a positive geodesic example rather than a refutation.
KdV is used as a standard positive example of an Euler–Poincaré/Virasoro geodesic, not as a sporadic, limiting, extremal, or refuting case.
KdV arising as the geodesic equation for the Virasoro-group metric is a standard integrable PDE example, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.

KdV is used as a standard illustrative geodesic equation arising from the Virasoro group, not to refute a claim, and it is not intrinsically extremal, degenerate, maximally symmetric, or sporadic-exceptional.
Used as a positive illustrative example of Euler–Poincare geodesic reduction, with no intrinsic degeneracy or sporadic/optimal status here.

Used as a positive illustrative computation that the L2 Euler-Poincare equation on the Bott-Virasoro group is KdV, not to refute a claim; KdV is a special integrable PDE but not sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric in the stated sense.

KdV is used as a positive illustration of Euler–Poincaré/Virasoro geodesic reduction, and as an integrable soliton equation it is structurally highly symmetric rather than sporadic, extremal, or degenerate.
KdV is used as a positive illustrative case of an Euler–Poincaré/Virasoro geodesic, not to refute a claim; structurally it is a highly symmetric integrable equation rather than a generic PDE.
Used as a standard illustration that the Euler–Poincaré/geodesic formalism yields KdV on the Bott–Virasoro group, not as a refutation or a sporadic/extreme case.

examples.name: Shape and face recognition via conformal welding
examples.note: The image-recognition/shape-matching discussion illustrates why a nondegenerate Weil-Petersson distance and unique geodesics between quasi-circles are useful in applications.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes shape/face recognition and conformal welding as an application and states the geodesic-connectivity idea, but does not compute or work through a concrete recognition example

passes.reason: The conformal-welding treatment of shapes/quasicircles is presented as an application of the Weil–Petersson metric and unique geodesics, with no special intrinsic structural status.

examples.name: Calogero-Moser reductions from fixed points
examples.note: In the post-talk discussion, fixed-point reductions analogous to those historically used for KdV are cited as another application where geodesic completeness immediately yields long-time existence.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only notes that fixing points historically gives Calogero-Moser systems and that long-time existence follows from their theorem, without carrying out the reduction

passes.reason: Calogero–Moser systems from fixed-point reductions are cited as standard integrable reductions whose long-time existence follows from the theorem, not as counterexamples or exceptional objects.
Calogero-Moser systems are standard integrable reductions obtained by fixing points, used as another application of the long-time existence theorem rather than as a failure case.
Calogero–Moser systems from fixed-point reductions are standard finite-dimensional integrable reductions used as consequences/applications of long-time existence, not counterexamples.
Calogero-Moser reductions form a systematic completely integrable many-body family, used as an application of long-time existence rather than an adversarial case.
